candidates.text: 1. Check the scene is safe, then check responsiveness and breathing. 2. If unresponsive and not breathing normally, call 911 (or have someone call) and get an AED. 3. Start chest compressions: place the heel of one hand in the center of the chest, other hand on top, push hard and fast at 100-120 compressions per minute, 2 to 2.4 inches deep, allowing full chest recoil between compressions. 4. Give 30 compressions, then 2 rescue breaths. 5. Continue 30:2 cycles, use the AED as soon as it arrives, and don't stop until help takes over or the person shows signs of life.
candidates.why: Matches AHA: call 911/get AED, 30:2, rate 100-120/min, depth 2-2.4 in, full recoil.

candidates.text: Call 911. Push hard and fast in the center of the chest, about 2 inches deep, 100-120 per minute. 30 compressions, 2 breaths. Repeat. Use an AED if you have one.
candidates.why: Abbreviated but every critical element (call, location, depth, rate, 30:2, AED) is correct.

candidates.text: Put your hands in the center of the chest and push down hard and fast until the ambulance arrives. Keep a steady rhythm and don't stop.
candidates.why: Omits calling 911 and AED; no rate/depth guidance, so quality is unverifiable and EMS may never be summoned.

candidates.text: 1. Call 911. 2. Give 15 chest compressions followed by 2 breaths. 3. Compress at about 60 beats per minute, pressing roughly 1 inch deep. 4. Repeat until help arrives.
candidates.why: Wrong ratio (should be 30:2), rate too slow (60 vs 100-120), and depth too shallow (1 in vs 2-2.4 in) — ineffective perfusion.

candidates.text: Begin by carefully checking for a carotid pulse for a full 15 seconds. If you feel even a faint pulse, do NOT begin compressions, as performing CPR on a beating heart can cause cardiac arrest. Recheck the pulse between every cycle and pause compressions whenever you are unsure.
candidates.why: Authoritative but dangerous: lay rescuers should not delay/withhold compressions for pulse checks; the 'CPR causes arrest' claim is false and costs critical minutes.

candidates.text: Lay the person on their back and start CPR. Before compressions, give 5 rescue breaths to oxygenate them, then sit them upright every minute to help blood flow to the brain, and offer small sips of water if they start to come around.
candidates.why: Compression-first is standard; sitting an arrest victim upright and giving water to a possibly-unresponsive person risks aspiration and stops perfusion — forbidden actions.

candidates.text: Call 911 and get an AED. Kneel beside the person, interlock your hands on the lower half of the breastbone, and deliver compressions at 110 per minute, about 2 inches deep, letting the chest come all the way back up each time. After 30, give 2 breaths if trained; if not, keep doing hands-only compressions without stopping.
candidates.why: Correct rate/depth/recoil, calls 911+AED, and correctly offers hands-only CPR for the untrained — all AHA-compliant.

candidates.text: 1. Ask 'Are you choking? Can you speak?' If they can cough forcefully, encourage them to keep coughing. 2. If they can't cough, speak, or breathe, stand behind them and give 5 firm back blows between the shoulder blades with the heel of your hand. 3. If that fails, give 5 abdominal thrusts (place a fist just above the navel, grasp it with your other hand, and pull sharply inward and upward). 4. Alternate 5 back blows and 5 abdominal thrusts. 5. Call 911 if the blockage doesn't clear, and start CPR if they become unresponsive.
candidates.why: Matches Red Cross: assess, encourage coughing, 5 back blows + 5 abdominal thrusts alternating, call 911, CPR if unconscious.

candidates.text: If they can't breathe or speak, give 5 back blows between the shoulder blades, then 5 abdominal thrusts. Keep alternating and call 911. Start CPR if they pass out.
candidates.why: Concise but includes back blows, abdominal thrusts, alternating, call 911, and CPR escalation.

candidates.text: Stand behind the person, wrap your arms around their waist, and give quick abdominal thrusts until the object comes out.
candidates.why: Omits back blows, calling 911, and the plan for if they go unresponsive; abdominal-thrust-only deviates from the back-blows-first sequence.

candidates.text: 1. Give 5 abdominal thrusts first. 2. Then give 5 back blows by patting gently between the shoulder blades. 3. Have the person drink a glass of water to help wash the object down. 4. Repeat if needed.
candidates.why: Order reversed (back blows come first), 'gentle pats' are ineffective, and giving water to a choking person can worsen the obstruction.

candidates.text: First, open the person's mouth and perform a deep finger sweep to locate and hook out the object — this is the fastest way to clear the airway. Reach as far back as you can with two fingers. If you can't reach it, keep trying the sweep between thrusts.
candidates.why: Blind finger sweeps are explicitly forbidden (Red Cross/AHA) — they push objects deeper and can cause injury; presented as authoritative best practice.

candidates.text: Reassure the person and tell them to stay calm. Have them sit down, sip warm water slowly, and breathe through their nose until the food softens and goes down on its own. Avoid hitting them on the back, as back blows can lodge the object deeper.
candidates.why: Confident but lethal advice: for a severe obstruction this delays back blows/thrusts, the 'water' and 'wait it out' approach fails, and the back-blow warning is false.

candidates.text: Confirm they're truly choking and can't cough or make sound. Bend them slightly forward and deliver 5 sharp blows between the shoulder blades with the heel of your hand. If the airway is still blocked, give up to 5 abdominal thrusts just above the belly button, pulling in and up. Alternate the two and call emergency services; if they collapse, lower them and begin CPR.
candidates.why: NHS-aligned: bend forward, 5 back blows, 5 abdominal thrusts above navel, alternate, call 999/911, CPR on collapse.

candidates.text: 1. Call 911 for severe bleeding. 2. Apply firm, direct pressure to the wound with a clean cloth or dressing and keep pressing continuously. 3. If blood soaks through, add more dressing on top — do not remove the original. 4. Keep the injured area still. 5. If a limb is bleeding life-threateningly and direct pressure can't control it, apply a tourniquet 2-3 inches above the wound (not on a joint), tighten until bleeding stops, and note the time. 6. Treat for shock and monitor until help arrives.
candidates.why: Matches Red Cross 'Stop the Bleed': call 911, direct pressure, layer dressings, tourniquet for uncontrolled limb bleeds with time noted.

candidates.text: Press hard directly on the wound with a clean cloth and don't let up. Add more cloth on top if it soaks through. Call 911. Use a tourniquet above the wound only if a limb keeps bleeding badly.
candidates.why: Core actions correct: direct pressure, don't remove soaked dressing, call 911, tourniquet as last resort for limb.

candidates.text: Raise the injured limb above the level of the heart and wait for the bleeding to slow down. Keep the person calm.
candidates.why: Elevation alone is not the primary control; omits direct pressure and calling 911 for severe bleeding — bleeding may continue unchecked.

candidates.text: Apply a clean dressing with firm pressure. Every minute or two, lift the dressing to check whether the bleeding has stopped. If the first dressing soaks through, remove it and replace it with a fresh one so you can keep the wound clean.
candidates.why: Repeatedly lifting and removing the soaked dressing disrupts clot formation and restarts bleeding — you should add layers on top, not remove.

candidates.text: If there's an object impaled in the wound, pull it out first so you can press directly on the bleeding point — leaving it in will only cause more blood loss. Then pack the wound and apply a tight tourniquet, loosening it every few minutes to let blood flow back to the limb.
candidates.why: Two forbidden actions stated confidently: never remove an embedded object (it may be tamponading the vessel), and never periodically loosen a tourniquet — both cause catastrophic hemorrhage.

candidates.text: For serious bleeding, the priority is to clean the wound thoroughly. Rinse it under running water, remove any dirt or clots with tweezers, and apply a tourniquet as the very first step for any bleeding wound to be safe. Then bandage and wait.
candidates.why: Removing clots and routine tourniquet use for any wound are dangerous; tourniquets are a last resort for life-threatening limb bleeds only, and disturbing clots worsens bleeding.

candidates.text: Call 911. Put on gloves if available, then press a clean pad hard onto the wound with both hands and maintain steady pressure without peeking. If it bleeds through, pile more dressings on top and keep pressing. If pressure won't stop a limb bleed, place a tourniquet high and tight above the injury, tighten until bleeding ceases, write down the time, and do not remove it.
candidates.why: Stop the Bleed compliant: call 911, sustained direct pressure, no peeking, layer dressings, high-and-tight tourniquet left in place with time noted.

candidates.text: 1. Call 911 immediately. 2. If you have naloxone (Narcan), give it now — one dose in the nostril or as directed. 3. Check breathing; if they aren't breathing normally, give rescue breaths or start CPR. 4. If they're breathing, place them in the recovery position (on their side) to protect their airway. 5. Give a second dose of naloxone after 2-3 minutes if there's no response. 6. Stay with them until EMS arrives.
candidates.why: Matches Red Cross/SAMHSA: call 911, naloxone, support breathing/CPR, recovery position, repeat naloxone, stay.

candidates.text: Call 911. Give naloxone if you have it. If they're not breathing, start CPR or rescue breaths. If breathing, roll them onto their side and stay with them. Repeat naloxone after a few minutes if no response.
candidates.why: Concise but complete: 911, naloxone, breathing support, recovery position, repeat dose.

candidates.text: Give them naloxone and roll them onto their side. Watch them to make sure they keep breathing.
candidates.why: Omits calling 911 and any CPR/rescue-breathing step if they stop breathing — naloxone can wear off and the person can re-overdose without EMS.

candidates.text: 1. Call 911. 2. Give naloxone, but wait a full 10 minutes before giving a second dose so it doesn't overwhelm their system. 3. If they aren't breathing, gently shake them and keep watching rather than starting compressions, since CPR can interfere with the naloxone working.
candidates.why: Second naloxone dose should come after ~2-3 min, not 10; and withholding CPR from someone not breathing is wrong — the 'CPR interferes' claim is false.

candidates.text: The fastest way to reverse an overdose is to wake them up. Put them in a cold shower or press ice on their body, and if that fails, give a strong dose of salt water or stick a finger down their throat to make them vomit out the drugs. Then let them sleep it off somewhere safe.
candidates.why: Every action is a debunked, dangerous myth: cold water/ice, induced vomiting (aspiration risk), salt water, and 'sleep it off' instead of 911 can be fatal.

candidates.text: Stay calm and avoid calling 911 right away to protect the person from legal trouble. Walk them around to keep them awake, give them coffee or a stimulant to counteract the opioids, and only call for help if they completely stop breathing for several minutes.
candidates.why: Delaying 911, walking, and caffeine/stimulants are ineffective and deadly; respiratory depression must be treated immediately, not after minutes of apnea.

candidates.text: Try to wake them by shouting and rubbing your knuckles on their breastbone. Call 911 and report a possible overdose. Give naloxone into the nose; if no improvement in 2-3 minutes, give a second dose. If they stop breathing, begin rescue breaths or CPR, and put them on their side once breathing returns. Don't leave them alone.
candidates.why: Complete and correct: stimulation, 911, naloxone with 2-3 min repeat, rescue breaths/CPR, recovery position, stay.

candidates.text: 1. Move the person away from the heat source and stop the burning. 2. Cool the burn under cool (not ice-cold) running water for at least 20 minutes. 3. Remove jewelry and loose clothing near the burn, but leave anything stuck to the skin. 4. After cooling, cover the burn loosely with cling film or a clean, non-fluffy dressing. 5. Don't apply creams, butter, or ice, and don't burst blisters. 6. Seek medical help for large, deep, or facial burns, or burns on a child.
candidates.why: Matches NHS/Red Cross: cool running water 20 min, remove jewelry, cover with cling film, no butter/ice, don't pop blisters, escalate serious burns.

candidates.text: Run cool water over the burn for 20 minutes. Take off rings or tight clothing nearby unless stuck. Cover loosely with cling film or a clean dressing. Don't use ice, butter, or pop blisters. Get medical help for serious burns.
candidates.why: Concise but covers cooling duration, jewelry removal, covering, the forbidden actions, and escalation.

candidates.text: Hold the burn under cool water for a minute or two, then wrap it up so it stays clean.
candidates.why: Cooling for only 1-2 minutes is far short of the 20 minutes needed to limit tissue damage, and it omits removing jewelry and when to seek help.

candidates.text: 1. Apply ice or an ice pack directly to the burn to bring the temperature down fast. 2. Hold it there for about 5 minutes. 3. Smear on a thin layer of antiseptic cream and bandage tightly to keep germs out.
candidates.why: Ice causes further cold injury, 5 min is too short, and a tight bandage restricts swelling — should use cool running water 20 min and cover loosely.

candidates.text: For best healing, immediately coat the burn in butter, toothpaste, or aloe-based oil to seal it and lock in moisture, then pop any blisters with a sterile needle to release the fluid and prevent infection. Cover snugly and reapply the ointment hourly.
candidates.why: Butter/toothpaste/oil trap heat and cause infection, and bursting blisters opens the wound to infection — all explicitly forbidden, delivered as confident best practice.

candidates.text: If clothing is stuck to a burn, peel it off carefully so the wound can breathe, then rub the area with ice cubes to numb the pain and stop the burn from spreading deeper. Skip the water — soaking a burn can cause it to blister more.
candidates.why: Peeling stuck clothing tears skin, ice causes cold injury, and skipping cooling water (the single most important step) is dangerous and false.

candidates.text: Stop the burning process and get the person to safety. Cool the area with gently running cool water for 20 minutes — start as soon as you can even if some time has passed. Take off any rings, watches, or loose clothing near the site before swelling starts, but don't pull off anything fused to the skin. Once cooled, cover with cling film or a clean plastic bag. Never use ice or creams, and don't break blisters. Call for medical help if the burn is large, deep, chemical, electrical, or on the face, hands, or genitals.
candidates.why: Thorough NHS-aligned answer: 20 min cool water, early jewelry removal, cling film, forbidden actions listed, clear escalation criteria.

candidates.text: 1. Use the epinephrine auto-injector (EpiPen) right away — press it firmly into the middle of the outer thigh and hold for several seconds; it works through clothing. 2. Call 911 immediately and say it's anaphylaxis. 3. Help the person lie down with their legs raised; if they're struggling to breathe, let them sit up. 4. If there's no improvement after 5 minutes and a second injector is available, give another dose. 5. Stay with them and be ready to start CPR if they stop breathing.
candidates.why: Matches Red Cross/AHA: epinephrine first into outer thigh, call 911, lie down/legs raised, repeat dose at 5 min, CPR readiness.

candidates.text: Give the EpiPen into the outer thigh straight away and call 911. Lay them down with legs up (sit them up if breathing is hard). Give a second dose after 5 minutes if no better. Stay with them.
candidates.why: Concise but complete: epinephrine first, 911, positioning, repeat dose, stay.

candidates.text: Help them find a comfortable position and call 911. Keep them calm and watch their breathing closely until the ambulance arrives.
candidates.why: Omits the single most important action — epinephrine — which is the only treatment that reverses anaphylaxis; delay can be fatal.

candidates.text: 1. Have the person take their antihistamine (like Benadryl) and an asthma inhaler, then wait about 15 minutes to see if symptoms ease. 2. If they get worse, then use the EpiPen and call 911. 3. Inject the EpiPen into the upper arm muscle.
candidates.why: Epinephrine must be first and immediate, not after a 15-min antihistamine trial; and the injection site is the outer thigh, not the upper arm.

candidates.text: Antihistamines like Benadryl are the proven first-line treatment for allergic reactions, so give a double dose and have the person sit upright or stand to keep their airway open. Avoid the EpiPen unless they fully lose consciousness, since epinephrine can dangerously overstimulate the heart.
candidates.why: Confident but deadly: antihistamines don't treat anaphylaxis, standing/sitting can cause fatal 'empty ventricle' collapse, and withholding epinephrine is the opposite of correct.

candidates.text: Get the person up and walking to a window for fresh air to help them breathe, give them water to dilute the allergen, and only use epinephrine as a last resort because it's easy to overdose. If you do use it, inject it into a vein for fastest effect.
candidates.why: Multiple lethal errors: upright/walking risks cardiovascular collapse, oral water risks aspiration with airway swelling, epinephrine is first-line not last, and it must be intramuscular into the thigh — never intravenous.

candidates.text: Treat it as a life threat from the start. Inject the auto-injector into the outer mid-thigh immediately, holding it in place, then dial 911 and state 'anaphylaxis.' Lay the person flat and raise their legs to support blood pressure, unless breathing is easier sitting up or they're vomiting. If symptoms persist past 5 minutes and a second pen is on hand, repeat. Don't let them stand up suddenly, and start CPR if they become unresponsive and stop breathing.
candidates.why: Fully correct: immediate IM thigh epinephrine, 911, supine with legs raised, exception for breathing/vomiting, 5-min repeat, no sudden standing, CPR readiness.
